entries.title: Comparer overview - visual diff between two pieces of data
entries.body:
# Burp Comparer - overview

**Comparer** is a Burp tool for visually comparing two pieces of data and highlighting the differences between them. In essence it is a "diff" inside Burp: you put two items into it (for example, two HTTP responses), and Comparer shows what exactly changed - where text was added, where it was deleted, where it was replaced. You can compare anything textual: requests, responses, individual values, arbitrary strings.

## Why this is needed

Very often the sign of a vulnerability is a **barely noticeable difference** between two responses that is hard to catch by eye in the flow. Login with a correct and an incorrect password, an existing and a non-existent user, a `TRUE` and a `FALSE` condition in a blind injection - outwardly the responses are almost identical, but one has a couple more words or a different status code. Comparer finds these discrepancies for you and colors them, turning "it seems something is different" into a precise list of changes.

## Classic use cases

* **Username enumeration.** Compare the response to a login attempt with an existing username and with a deliberately non-existent one. If the application responds differently (different error text, length, redirect) - this is an oracle by which valid usernames are brute-forced.
* **Blind injection (blind SQLi / boolean-based).** You send a condition that is true and a condition that is false. Compared the two responses: there is a stable difference -> the application "gives itself away" by a boolean trait, the injection is confirmed.
* **Analyzing Intruder results.** After a brute-force attack you take two responses with a noticeably different length and compare them to understand *what exactly* makes the "interesting" response different - a successful bypass, a data leak, a different message.
* **Authentication bypass / difference in privileges.** Comparing the response of a privileged and an ordinary user to the same request helps see which data/elements are available only to the privileged one.
* **Any "before/after".** Compare a request before and after editing by session handling rules, two response variants under different headers, and so on.

## What Comparer shows

The comparison result opens in a separate window with **two panels** (the first item on the left, the second on the right). Differences are highlighted by color by type: what is **modified**, what is **deleted**, what is **added**. Text without highlighting is present identically in both items. The window title shows the **total number of differences** - a quick indicator of "strongly or weakly different".

## Two comparison modes

Comparer can compare at two levels of detail:

* **Compare word** - word-by-word comparison. The data is split into tokens by whitespace separators, and the minimal edits at the word level are found. This is the most convenient mode for HTTP responses: you see meaningful differences (a different word, a different value) without noise on every character.
* **Compare byte** - byte-by-byte comparison. Edits at the level of individual bytes/characters are found. The mode is more precise but "noisier" and requires noticeably more computation; useful when the tiniest differences matter (a single character, binary data).

## Editions

Comparer is available in both **Community Edition** and **Professional** - there are no edition restrictions.

## Quick start

```
1. Right-click the first response (for example, a login with a correct username) > Send to Comparer.
2. Right-click the second response (login with a non-existent username) > Send to Comparer.
3. Open the Comparer tab - both items are already in the tables.
4. Select one item in each table.
5. Click Compare words (or Compare bytes).
6. In the window that opens, read the highlighted differences.
```

entries.title: Working with Comparer - loading data, modes, and reading differences
entries.body:
# Working with Comparer

This section is a hands-on walkthrough of the full cycle: how to load two items, select them, run the comparison in the right mode, and correctly read the highlighted result.

## Step 1. Load the data

Comparer keeps a list of items ready for comparison. You can put data there in three ways:

* **Send to Comparer** - the most common path. Right-click a request or response in any Burp tool (Proxy > HTTP history, Repeater, Logger, Intruder results) and choose **Send to Comparer**. The item is added to the list.
* **Paste** - paste data from the clipboard with the **Paste** button (handy for arbitrary strings copied from anywhere).
* **Load** - load data from a file with the **Load** button.

For a meaningful comparison you need at least **two** items. Usually you send exactly two "opponents": response A and response B, between which you look for the difference.

## Step 2. Select two items

Comparer has two item tables. Comparison works between **two** selected items: you select one item in the first table (it becomes the "left", original one) and one in the second (it becomes the "right", the one being compared against). Extra items can be removed with the delete buttons: **Remove** (remove the selected one) and **Clear** (clear the whole list).

## Step 3. Run the comparison in the right mode

Two buttons are available at the bottom, setting the level of detail:

* **Compare words** (word-by-word) - take this by default for HTTP requests and responses. The data is tokenized by whitespace, and Burp looks for differences at the word level. The result is readable: it highlights exactly the meaningful discrepancies, not every differing character.
* **Compare bytes** (byte-by-byte) - when the tiniest differences matter (a single character in a token, binary data, non-displayable bytes). The mode is more precise but produces more "jitter" and requires more computational resources, so on large data it is slower.

The rule for choosing is simple: start with **Compare words**; switch to **Compare bytes** only if word-level resolution is not enough.

## Step 4. Read the result

A comparison window opens with two panels (the first item on the left, the second on the right). Differences are highlighted by color by three types of edits:

* **modified** - the fragment is present in both items but differs;
* **deleted** - the fragment is present only in the first (left) item;
* **added** - the fragment is present only in the second (right) item.

Text **without highlighting** is identical in both items. The window title shows the **total number of differences** - from it you immediately see the scale of the discrepancy: a couple of differences versus dozens.

## Useful elements of the comparison window

* **Sync views** - synchronize the scrolling of both panels: you scroll one - the second follows along, and the corresponding spots are always opposite each other. On long responses turn this on without fail, otherwise the panels "drift apart".
* **Text / Hex** - a toggle for the panel display mode. **Text** - for readable data; **Hex** - when you need to compare exact bytes (especially together with **Compare bytes**), see non-displayable characters or differences in binary data.

## End-to-end example: username enumeration

```
1. In the application, make two login attempts:
   - with a deliberately existing user + a wrong password;
   - with a deliberately non-existent user + any password.
2. In Proxy > HTTP history find both responses.
3. Right-click each > Send to Comparer.
4. On the Comparer tab select these two responses (one in each table).
5. Click Compare words.
6. Turn on Sync views and read the highlighting.
   - If the responses differ (different error text, length, redirect) -
     you have an oracle: by this difference valid usernames are enumerated.
   - If there is no difference - the application responds identically, enumeration
     by this trait does not work.
```

## Practice and pitfalls

* **Compare comparable responses.** For the difference to be telling, two requests should differ **only** by the factor being checked (user exists/not; condition true/false). If the requests also differ by something extraneous (a different CSRF token, time, a random nonce), those differences will also be highlighted and add noise to the picture - mentally (or explicitly) discard such "technical" jitter.
* **Words versus bytes.** On "human" responses **Compare words** is clearer. Resort to **Compare bytes** when you need to catch a single-character difference or compare a binary - but be ready for a noisier and slower result.
* **Comparer is an analyzer, not a sender.** On its own it sends nothing to the server. The typical flow: you make and repeat requests in **Repeater** (or brute-force in **Intruder**), and send interesting responses with **Send to Comparer** to understand precisely *how* they differ.
* **Pairing with Intruder.** After a brute-force attack, sort the results by response length, take the response that is the size "outlier" and a reference one - and compare them in Comparer to see the concrete cause of the anomaly.
